reasoning: The finding claims an open redirect via wp_redirect() at lines 122-127 where $this->formkey (from $_GET['formkey']) is embedded in the URL. This is false for two independent reasons. First, in the constructor (lines 36-38), $this->formkey is validated with a strict in_array(..., true) check against the server-controlled whitelist of registered post type slugs; any value not in the list causes an immediate exit() before any actions (_save included) are ever registered — the attacker cannot reach the sink with an arbitrary value. Second, even if the whitelist were bypassed, admin_url() wraps the entire redirect target: the final URL is 'https://example.com/wp-admin/edit.php?post_type=mw-wp-form&page=mw-wp-form-chart&formkey=<formkey>'. The attacker-controlled formkey value is only a query-parameter value within a URL already rooted at the local admin origin; it cannot redirect to an off-site host. The wp_redirect vs wp_safe_redirect distinction is only exploitable when an attacker controls the full URL passed to wp_redirect, not a query-string parameter embedded inside admin_url(). The filter-injection hypothesis (mwform_contact_data_post_types) would at most inject a strange post-type slug into the whitelist; admin_url() still anchors the redirect to the local site. No exploitable open redirect exists.
